era_context: Late Bronze Age, Egypt's 18th Dynasty under Amenhotep III at the height of his power. The Mycenaean world is in LHIIIA; Mitanni and Egypt maintain a balance of power in the Levant; the Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I is beginning to expand. Shang Dynasty in China continues its mature phase.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign; construction and consolidation of power
events.description: Amenhotep III continues to consolidate Egypt's power and wealth. This period sees the construction of major temples and monuments, including the Colossi of Memnon and the Temple of Luxor. Egypt's economy is bolstered by tribute from Nubia and the Levant, and the pharaoh maintains a vast harem and court. His reign is characterized by relative peace and prosperity, with military campaigns conducted to maintain control over vassal territories rather than to expand the empire.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions at Luxor and Karnak; administrative records and correspondence
Archaeological evidence from Malkata palace and temple complexes

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-documented through monumental inscriptions and archaeological evidence. The year 1378 BCE is assigned by conventional Egyptological dating with ±5–10 year uncertainty.

events.title: Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I; early expansion in Anatolia
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I (r. c. 1344–1322 BCE) is in the early phase of his reign, during which he will expand Hittite power throughout Anatolia and into Syria. By this date, he is consolidating control over Anatolia and beginning campaigns that will eventually challenge Egyptian dominance in the Levant. The Hittites are known for their iron-working technology and military innovations, including the use of chariots.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and annals; cuneiform tablets from Boğazköy (Hattusa)
Archaeological evidence from Hattusa and other Hittite sites

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is attested in Hittite records, but the exact chronology is debated. His reign is traditionally dated c. 1344–1322 BCE, but some scholars place it earlier or later by 10–20 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Šuppiluliuma I's reign is contested. Some scholars place his reign earlier (c. 1360–1330 BCE) or later (c. 1330–1300 BCE) depending on the chronological system used. The exact year 1378 BCE is assigned by convention but carries significant uncertainty. Additionally, the extent of Hittite expansion by this date is debated; some sources suggest Šuppiluliuma's major campaigns occurred later in his reign.
historiographic_note: Egyptian records are abundant and detailed, but they reflect the pharaonic perspective and may downplay military setbacks or exaggerate achievements. Hittite records are also available but are fragmentary and often compiled after the events they describe. The perspective is heavily focused on elite political and military history; economic and social history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III and Šuppiluliuma I are contemporaries; their reigns will eventually lead to conflict over control of the Levant.
